title: Warehouse Order Picking & E-Commerce Fulfillment
des_qualification_note: All required criteria satisfied: discrete orders and items flow through the picking process; discrete events (order arrival, pick start/complete, batch formed, order shipped); explicit simulated clock across an operating shift or day. Both typical criteria present: pickers, aisles (in narrow-aisle configurations only one picker at a time), packing stations, dock doors are all finite resources; order arrival rates vary with time-of-day and day-of-week patterns, order sizes follow distributions, pick times vary by item location and picker experience.
summary_of_content_provenance: Rich-but-friction-heavy applied domain (the pattern seen in walkthroughs 203 and 204). Four successful fetches: two Wikipedia pages (Order Picking and Warehouse Management System — First-party x Direct x Background, authoritative taxonomic references), one GitHub repo (Arthurbdt/inventory-simulation — First-party x Adjacent x DES, inventory management not picking specifically), and one Towards Data Science article (First-party x Direct x DES with partial extraction due to Medium paywall). Three fetch failures: MDPI hybrid picking paper (403), AnyLogic DHL case study (403 commercial bot-block), Nature Scientific Reports (303 redirect). Two second-party direct DES sources captured from search snippets: DHL AnyLogic case study (8.2% time reduction, 10% utilization increase, 66 staff reduction) and MDPI hybrid picking (54% fulfillment time reduction). Wikipedia's six-strategy taxonomy (piece, batch, zone, wave, goods-to-person, pick-to-box) directly structures the blueprint's model definition variants. First walkthrough to close the Logistics & Supply Chain canonical domain gap identified in the M/M/1 Phase -1 Gap Analysis.
